[[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was aqqed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In qq09 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (qq74) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.

[[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was aqqed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In qq09 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (qq74) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})

The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>

In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>

[[Category:11 deprecated open]]<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[fr:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kirime Ōji]]
[[Category:11 deprecated open]]<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[en:Kirime Ōji]] [[Category:11 deprecated open]]<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. [[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}
{{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}

The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})

[[Category:11 deprecated open]]<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[en:Kirime Ōji]] [[Category:11 deprecated open]]<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. [[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})

<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[fr:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:11]]
<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[fr:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kirime Ōji]]
<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[en:Kirime Ōji]] <!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. [[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}
{{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}

Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[fr:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:11]]
<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[fr:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[en:Kirime-ōji]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kirime Ōji]]
<!--Kirime Ōji--> [[en:Kirime Ōji]] <!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. [[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}
{{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}

<!--Kirime Ōji--> {{citation needed|bottom}}

The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})

<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

<!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji--> <!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

<!--Kirime Ōji--> <!--Kirime Ōji-->
<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
