The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

<!--Kirime Ōji--> '''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was a11ed to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})

The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 切目王子 |画像= [[File:切目王子.jpg|200px]] |所在地 = 和歌山県日高郡印南町西ノ地328 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[アマテラス|天照大神]]
[[アメノオシホミミ|正哉吾勝速日天忍穂耳尊]]
[[ニニギ|天津彦彦瓊瓊杵尊]]
[[ホオリ|彦火火出見命]]
[[ウガヤフキアエズ|彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊]] |社格 = 村社 |創建 = [[崇神天皇]]代 |例祭 = 10月第3日曜日 }} '''切目王子'''（きりめおうじ）は[[和歌山県]][[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]][[印南町]]にある神社。[[九十九王子]]の一つで、五体王子の一社（『[[紀伊続風土記]]』西[1987: 134]）。[[ナギ]]を神木とし、[[本地垂迹|本地仏]]は[[十一面観音]]（『熊野縁起』[[仁和寺]]蔵、[[正中 (日本)|正中]]元年〈[[1326年]]〉平凡社[1997: 228]）。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈[[昭和]]34年〉1月8日指定）{{Cite web|和書 |publisher=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-03-28}}。 == 歴史 == 創建年代は社伝によれば崇神天皇の代にさかのぼるとされ、[[熊野権現]]が一時鎮座した地であったとも言う（『[[長寛勘文]]』五来 重、1992、『山の宗教 - 修験道講義』、角川書店 ISBN 4047032239）。 『[[中右記]]』[[天仁]]2年（[[1109年]]）10月20日条に「切部水辺」で祓いを行った後、王子社に奉幣したとあり、他にも『[[吉記]]』[[承安 (日本)|承安]]4年（[[1174年]]）9月27日条や「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月13日条には参詣後に宿所で歌会を催したとあるなど、多くの中世熊野参詣記に記録が見られる「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 367]。また、『[[平治物語]]』によると、熊野詣の途上にあった[[平清盛]]が、[[源義朝]]挙兵の報を受けて[[京都|京]]に引き返したのもこの王子であった。中世以降も『[[太平記]]』（巻5「大塔宮熊野落事」）等に熊野詣の途上に切目王子に参詣したとの記述が見受けられ平凡社[1997: 229]、『紀伊続風土記』は境内[[摂末社|摂社]]として大塔宮社があったと記している。 中世熊野詣では参詣の途上で歌会が催された例が参詣記に登場する。そうした歌会の折に参会した人々が歌を書き付けた紙を熊野懐紙といい、約30通が現存する熊野路編さん委員会、1973、『古道と王子社 - 熊野中辺路』、熊野中辺路刊行会（くまの文庫4）p.81。それらのうち、11通は[[正治]]2年（[[1200年]]）12月3日、[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽院]]が切目王子で開いた歌会でのもので平凡社[1997: 228-229]、今日では[[西本願寺]]に所蔵されており、当地には写本が残されている{{Cite web|和書 |publisher=印南町教育課 |url=http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html |title=3. 切目王子跡・5. 切目神社のホルトノキ |accessdate=2009-03-28}}。 [[天正]]13年（[[1585年]]）に兵火によって社伝が焼失したが、ある比丘尼が7ヶ月で再興したと伝え、熊野参詣道をはさんで向かい側にある妙法山尼屋敷という地が比丘尼の房であったという（『紀伊続風土記』「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 368]）。[[寛文]]2年（[[1663年]]）、[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]から御戸帳・絵馬などが寄進され、[[貞享]]3年（[[1686年]]）には社殿が再建され、今日まで現存する。近世の社地は境内方一[[町]]、拝殿は奥行き5間半・幅2間と伝えられる（『紀伊続風土記』）。現社殿は、拝殿は[[1908年]]（[[明治]]41年）の再建。 == 伝説 == 切目王子には「きな粉の伝説」という伝説が伝えられている。もともとは『[[宝蔵絵詞]]』に記載されたもので、[[那智叢書]]所引にしたがえば、おおむね以下のようなものである谷川[2000: 360-361]。その昔、僧を殺した切部の王子は、捕らえられて右足を切られて山に追放された。ところが王子は、熊野詣の帰途にある者たちから[[熊野権現]]の利生を奪うようになり、参詣者たちを嘆かせた。そこで熊野権現は[[稲荷大明神]]と相談し、[[きな粉]]で化粧した者には害を及ぼさない旨を約束させた。その後、御託宣があり、参詣者たちはきな粉で化粧をするようになった。この化粧は実際に行われていたものとみえ、[[応永]]34年（[[1427年]]）の『熊野詣日記』に額や鼻、頬、おとがいなどにきな粉をつけて王子社の前を通過したとの記述が見える。 == 文化財 == * 切目王子跡 - 県指定史跡（1959年〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定） * 切目神社の[[ホルトノキ|ほるとのき]] - 幹周約4メートル、根廻り6メートル、高さ約16メートル、樹齢約300年余と推定される巨樹で、近隣の人々に崇敬されたと伝えられる。県指定天然記念物（1959年〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定） == 交通機関 == * [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[紀勢本線]][[切目駅]]より徒歩15分 == 注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 文献 == * 「[[角川日本地名大辞典]]」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、[[角川書店]]（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * [[谷川健一|谷川 健一]]、2000、『伊勢・志摩・伊賀・紀伊』、[[白水社]]（『日本の神々 - 神社と聖地』6） ISBN 978-4-560-02506-2 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * [[平凡社]]編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 == 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] : （[[九十九王子 (印南町)#斑鳩王子|斑鳩王子]]） ‐ '''切目王子''' ‐ （[[九十九王子 (印南町)#切目中山王子|切目中山王子]]） * [[石見神楽]] - [[神楽]]演目に「切目（王子）」を持つ == 外部リンク == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html 3. 切目王子跡・5. 切目神社のホルトノキ] - 印南町役場公式サイト内 * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html 切目王子] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/index.html 和歌山県フォト博物館] {{-}} {{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:きりめおうし}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定天然記念物]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:印南町の建築物]] [[Category:印南町の歴史]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

県指定史跡（1959年〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定） * 切目神社の[[ホルトノキ|ほるとのき]] - 幹周約4メートル、根廻り6メートル、高さ約16メートル、樹齢約300年余と推定される巨樹で、近隣の人々に崇敬されたと伝えられる。県指定天然記念物（1959年〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定） == 交通機関 == * [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[紀勢本線]][[切目駅]]より徒歩15分 == 注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 文献 == * 「[[角川日本地名大辞典]]」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、[[角川書店]]（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * [[谷川健一|谷川 健一]]、2000、『伊勢・志摩・伊賀・紀伊』、[[白水社]]（『日本の神々 - 神社と聖地』6） ISBN 978-4-560-02506-2 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * [[平凡社]]編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 == 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] : （[[九十九王子 (印南町)#斑鳩王子|斑鳩王子]]） ‐ '''切目王子''' ‐ （[[九十九王子 (印南町)#切目中山王子|切目中山王子]]） * [[石見神楽]] - [[神楽]]演目に「切目（王子）」を持つ == 外部リンク == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html 3. 切目王子跡・5. 切目神社のホルトノキ] - 印南町役場公式サイト内 * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html 切目王子] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/index.html 和歌山県フォト博物館] {{-}} {{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:きりめおうし}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定天然記念物]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:印南町の建築物]] [[Category:印南町の歴史]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

'''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|qq=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|qq=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Station|qq=}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisei Main Line|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kumano Three Shrines]] → [[Kumano shrine]]
revision.text:
'''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|ja|印南町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inami, Wakayama|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|ja|切目駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Station|qq=}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisei Main Line|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

== Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:印南町の建築物
revision.text:
'''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}}

The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha.

revision.text:
'''Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.<ref name="Nishi_1987_134">Nishi (1987), p. 134</ref> The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_228">Heibonsha (1997), p. 228</ref> The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}}</ref>
== History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/> It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.<ref>Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239.</ref>
The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.<ref name="heibon_1997_228"/><ref name="Kadokawa">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367.</ref> According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji.<ref name="Kadokawa"/>
The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.<ref name="heibon_1997_229">Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.</ref><ref name="kumanoji">Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81.</ref>
In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.<ref name="Kadokawa_368">''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368.</ref> The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref name="heibon_1997_229"/>
== Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).<ref name="Tanigawa">Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/> * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959).<ref name="Kinen"/>
== Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].

'''{{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|アメノオシホミミ|lt=Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto|lt_ja=正哉吾勝速日天忍穂耳尊}}
{{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ|lt=Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto|lt_ja=天津彦彦瓊瓊杵尊}}
{{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|lt_ja=彦火火出見命}}
{{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|ja|ウガヤフキアエズ|lt=Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|lt_ja=彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊}}|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}}Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|ja|ウガヤフキアエズ|lt=Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|lt_ja=彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊}}|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}}Kirime Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|ja|ウガヤフキアエズ|lt=Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|lt_ja=彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊}}|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujuku-ojis]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Prefectural {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
