[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]

[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} {{Ninety-Nine Ōji}} | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#比井王子|比井王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} '''[[sn:Kirime Ōji]]''' '''[[sn:-てんふれえと]]''' '''[[fr:Kirime-ōji]]''' '''[[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]''' '''[[ja:郡戸王子]]''' '''[[zh:郡戸王子]]''' '''[[sn:irime Ōji]]''' '''[[Category:印南町の建築物]]''' '''[[Category:Municipal History of Inami]]''' '''[[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:和歌山県指定天然記念物]]''' [[ja:切目王子]]

Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} '''[[sn:Kirime Ōji]]''' '''[[sn:-てんふれえと]]''' '''[[fr:Kirime-ōji]]''' '''[[ja:%E5%88%87%E7%9B%AE%E7%8E%8B%E5%AD%90]]''' '''[[ja:郡戸王子]]''' '''[[zh:郡戸王子]]''' [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto [[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11643221]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|lt=Kumano faith|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] '''[[zh:郡戸王子]]''' '''[[sn:irime Ōji]]''' '''[[Category:Buildings in Inami Town]]''' '''[[Category:Municipal History of Inami]]''' '''[[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:Natural Monuments Designated by Wakayama Prefecture]]''' [[ja:切目王子]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]
[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated historical sites|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} {{Ninety-Nine Ōji}} | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#比井王子|比井王子]] | [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == *
