[[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} {{Ninety-Nine Ōji}} | style="background:#ccffcc" '''[[zh:郡戸王子]]''' '''''' '''[[Category:Buildings in Inami Town]]''' '''[[Category:Municipal History of Inami]]''' '''[[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]]''' '''[[Category:Natural Monuments Designated by Wakayama Prefecture]]''' [[ja:切目王子]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q11643221}}

The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Coord|33|48|9.5|N|135|13|54|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} {{Ninety-Nine Ōji}} | style="background:#ccffcc" [[:en:Ugayafukiaezu|Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto]]|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Inami, Wakayama|Inami, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from [[:en:Kirime Station|Kirime Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}}|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|en|Inami, Wakayama|WD=Q1203734|lt=Inami, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|en|Kirime Station|WD=Q2898692|lt=Kirime Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} – Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} [[co:commons:Category:Kirime Ōji]][[fr:Kirime-ōji]][[ja:切目王子]] [[co:commons:Category:Kirime Ōji]][[fr:Kirime-ōji]][[ja:切目王子]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kirime Ōji]][[fr:Kirime-ōji]][[ja:切目王子]] {{infobox religious building|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}|image=切目王子.jpg|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
{{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Masakaagatsuhaya Ameno-oshihomimi no Mikoto}}
{{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsuhikohiko Ninigi no Mikoto}}
{{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}}
{{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagaremu Uzubiko Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}|name=藤白神社|location={{ill|和歌山県|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/和歌山県|qq=}}{{ill|海南市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/海南市|qq=}}藤白466|coordinates={{Coord|34|8|39.5dd|N|135|12|33.8dd|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=藤白神社}}|Rank=旧{{ill|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/県社|qq=}}|established=the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}}|main hall=三間社{{ill|流造|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/流造|qq=}}|alternative name={{Ublist| 藤白若一王子権現 | 藤代王子 | 藤白権現 }}|festival=馬角神事、七夕祭|map={{location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=藤白神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}}|prefecture={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}}} Kirime Ōji '''is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|fr|Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].''' == Kirime Ōji == '''Kirime Ōji''' (切目王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Inami, Wakayama|en|Inami, Wakayama|WD=Q1203734|lt=Inami, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the auxiliary shrines known as the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji}}, associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kodō|熊野古道}} pilgrimage routes. Kirime Ōji is considered one of the Five Main Ōji Shrines (五体王子, ''Gotai Ōji'') as referenced in the ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}''.Nishi (1987), p. 134 The shrine’s sacred tree is a {{nihongo|Nagi tree|ナギ}}, and its {{nihongo|honji suijaku|本地垂迹}} deity is the {{nihongo|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|十一面観音}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 228 The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-28}} == History == According to shrine tradition, Kirime Ōji dates back to the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is believed that {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} was temporarily enshrined here.Gorai Shigeru (1992), ''Yama no Shūkyō – Shugendō Kōgi'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 4047032239. The shrine appears in various medieval pilgrimage records. In 1109 (Tennin 2), the ''{{nihongo|Chūyūki|中右記}}'' notes that a purification ceremony was held at ''Kirime Mizube,'' followed by offerings at the shrine. The ''{{nihongo|Yoshiki|吉記}}'' (1174) and ''{{nihongo|Meigetsuki|明月記}}'' (1201) mention poetry gatherings held at Kirime Ōji.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 367. According to the ''{{nihongo|Heiji Monogatari|平治物語}}'', Taira no Kiyomori received news of Minamoto no Yoshitomo's rebellion while en route to Kumano and turned back to Kyoto from Kirime Ōji. The shrine also played a role in poetry culture. In 1200 (Shōji 2), Emperor Go-Toba held a poetry gathering at Kirime Ōji, producing eleven surviving poems known as ''Kumano Kaishi,'' now housed in {{nihongo|Nishi Hongan-ji|西本願寺}}.Heibonsha (1997), p. 229.Kumanoji Editorial Committee (1973), ''Kodō to Ōji-sha – Kumano Nakahechi'', Kumano Nakahechi Publishing, p. 81. In 1585 (Tenshō 13), the shrine was destroyed by fire during warfare but was swiftly rebuilt by a nun within seven months.''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), p. 368. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1686 (Jōkyō 3), with the main hall rebuilt in 1908 (Meiji 41). == Legends == Kirime Ōji is associated with the ''Kinako Legend,'' which tells of an outlaw deity who attacked pilgrims. To appease him, Kumano Gongen negotiated with {{nihongo|Inari Daimyōjin|稲荷大明神}}, promising that those wearing {{nihongo|kinako|きな粉}} (roasted soybean flour) as face powder would be spared. Pilgrims adhered to this practice, as documented in the ''Kumano Pilgrimage Diary'' of 1427 (Ōei 34).Tanigawa (2000), pp. 360–361. == Cultural Properties == * '''Kirime Ōji Site''' – Designated as a Wakayama Prefectural Historic Site (1959). * '''Holm Oak of Kirime Shrine''' – A giant tree estimated to be over 300 years old, revered by locals. Designated as a Prefectural Natural Monument (1959). == Access == * '''Train:''' 15-minute walk from {{ill|Kirime Station|en|Kirime Station|WD=Q2898692|lt=Kirime Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}. == Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten. * Tanigawa, Ken'ichi (2000), ''Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii'', Hakusuisha. * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Kumano Kodō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō. * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Daigo & Kii Temples and Shrines Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano shrine|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji}} : ({{nihongo|Ikaruga Ōji|斑鳩王子}}) – '''Kirime Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Kirime Nakayama Ōji|切目中山王子}}) ==References== {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.inami.wakayama.jp/kyoiku/kirimeouji.html Kirime Ōji Site – Official Website of Inami Town] * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/063.html Kirime Ōji – Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum] {{Shinto-stub}} (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> {{translated page|ja|切目王子|version=97906198|comment=Imported full ja history}} '''''' '''''' '''[[fr:Kirime-ōji]]''' '''''' '''[[ja:郡戸王子]]''' '''[[zh:郡戸王子]]''' '''''' ''''''
